items.title: Trump signs presidential memorandum on vehicle 'right to repair'
items.summary: The White House said the president signed a memorandum aimed at expanding Americans' ability to repair their own vehicles.
items.details: On June 29, 2026, the White House announced a presidential memorandum described as expanding what it called Americans' 'freedom to fix' their own vehicles. The action was among several signed at the White House that day.

items.title: USDA announces beef processing support program
items.summary: The U.S. Department of Agriculture announced a program to provide temporary support for eligible beef processing establishments.
items.details: On June 30, 2026, Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins announced the Strengthening Processing for U.S. Ranchers (SPUR) Program. The department described it as providing temporary support for eligible beef processing establishments.

items.title: Proclamation suspends certain phosphate fertilizer duties
items.summary: The president signed a proclamation temporarily suspending countervailing duties on certain phosphate fertilizer imports.
items.details: On June 29, 2026, the administration announced a proclamation temporarily suspending countervailing duties on certain phosphate fertilizer imports. USDA described the action as part of a broader agricultural strategy.

items.title: U.S. and Iran hold indirect talks in Doha
items.summary: Mediators concluded separate meetings with U.S. and Iranian negotiators in Doha, with talks set to continue later.
items.details: On July 1, 2026, Qatar and Pakistan mediators concluded separate meetings with U.S. and Iranian negotiators in Doha. Qatar's foreign ministry said the parties agreed to continue discussions, with the next meeting to be scheduled after funeral processions for Iran's former supreme leader.

items.title: U.S. and Iran agree to halt strikes over Strait of Hormuz dispute
items.summary: U.S. officials said both sides agreed to stop attacks amid a dispute over the Strait of Hormuz.
items.details: On June 28, 2026, a senior U.S. official said the U.S. and Iran agreed to stop attacking each other and hold talks in Qatar. The renewed fighting had been sparked by competing interpretations of a memorandum of understanding on the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: United States and Cambodia sign air transport agreement
items.summary: The State Department announced a civil air transport agreement between the United States and Cambodia.
items.details: On July 1, 2026, the State Department announced that the United States and Cambodia signed an air transport agreement. The department said the agreement aligns the aviation relationship with U.S. Open Skies principles.

items.title: House advances fiscal 2027 defense authorization bill
items.summary: The House Rules Committee reported a rule for the fiscal 2027 National Defense Authorization Act, though a related floor resolution failed.
items.details: The Rules Committee reported a structured rule for H.R. 8800, the fiscal 2027 NDAA, by an 8-4 vote on June 29, 2026. On June 30, the House did not agree to the accompanying resolution on a 198-224 vote. The committee-passed bill endorsed about $1.15 trillion for defense programs.

items.title: Congress passes housing affordability legislation
items.summary: Both chambers passed a housing affordability bill; the president said he would delay a signing ceremony pending other legislation.
items.details: The House cleared a housing affordability bill on June 23, 2026, following Senate passage. Provisions include streamlining certain federal financing regulations and a contested measure related to institutional investors. The president canceled a planned signing ceremony, saying Congress must first pass a voter ID measure.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on birthright citizenship executive order
items.summary: The Supreme Court struck down the executive order seeking to end automatic birthright citizenship for certain children born in the U.S.
items.details: On June 30, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled that children born in the U.S. to parents in the country unlawfully or temporarily present are citizens at birth under the 14th Amendment. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the opinion; three justices dissented.

items.title: Supreme Court upholds mail-in ballot grace period
items.summary: In a 5-4 ruling, the Court upheld a Mississippi law allowing counting of mail ballots received after Election Day if postmarked by then.
items.details: On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 5-4 that Mississippi's law permitting counting of mail ballots received up to five days after Election Day, if postmarked beforehand, does not conflict with federal law. Justice Amy Coney Barrett wrote the opinion, rejecting an RNC challenge.

items.title: Supreme Court strikes down coordinated party spending limits
items.summary: In a 6-3 ruling, the Court struck down federal limits on party spending coordinated with candidates.
items.details: On June 30, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that federal limits on coordinated party expenditures violate the First Amendment. The case stemmed from a Republican-led lawsuit; the decision overruled a 2001 precedent that had upheld the limits.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on presidential removal power
items.summary: The Court ruled that the president may remove members of independent agencies, while allowing a Federal Reserve governor to remain for now.
items.details: On June 29, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that President Trump's 2025 firing of an FTC commissioner without cause was lawful. In a separate 5-4 ruling, the Court found that Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook could stay in her position for now.

items.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama to use congressional map for midterms
items.summary: The Court allowed Alabama to use a 2023 congressional map that a lower court had previously barred.
items.details: In an unsigned order issued in late June 2026, the Supreme Court allowed Alabama to use its preferred congressional map for the 2026 elections. The map, originally drawn in 2023, had earlier been blocked by a federal court. Reports say it reduces the number of majority-Black districts.

items.title: Where the 2026 redistricting battle stands
items.summary: With the general election months away, mid-decade redistricting fights across roughly ten states are settling for 2026.
items.details: Multiple states enacted new congressional maps during the 2026 cycle, including Texas, California, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio and Utah. Reporting indicates the map-drawing battles for 2026 are largely concluding, with several states now looking toward the 2028 cycle.

CA.title: California announces balanced 2026-27 budget agreement
CA.summary: The governor and legislative leaders announced a 2026-27 state budget agreement with no deficit.
CA.details: On June 26, 2026, Governor Gavin Newsom, Senate President pro Tempore Monique Limón and Assembly Speaker Robert Rivas announced a three-party agreement on California's 2026-27 budget. Officials described it as a balanced plan with zero deficit that funds healthcare and other services.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

GA.title: Georgia lawmakers decline to redraw maps in special session
GA.summary: Legislative leaders said they would not take up redistricting during a special session called by the governor.
GA.details: On June 17, 2026, Georgia House and Senate Republican leaders told Governor Brian Kemp they would not redraw congressional or legislative maps during the special session, citing pending litigation. Kemp had called the session after the Supreme Court's Louisiana v. Callais decision.

NY.title: New York legislature advances redistricting amendment
NY.summary: The Democratic-led legislature passed the first approval of a constitutional amendment allowing mid-decade redistricting for 2028.
NY.details: On June 3, 2026, the New York state legislature approved first passage of a constitutional amendment that would permit mid-decade redistricting and alter the independent commission's role. The amendment must pass again next year and be approved by voters before taking effect.

MS.title: Mississippi governor responds to mail-ballot ruling
MS.summary: Governor Tate Reeves said the state legislature should change its law after the Supreme Court upheld its mail-ballot grace period.
MS.details: After the June 29, 2026 Supreme Court ruling upholding Mississippi's mail-ballot grace period, Governor Tate Reeves said the state legislature should repeal the law and require mail-in ballots to be received by 5 p.m. on Election Day to be counted.

CT.title: Connecticut enacts youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: The governor signed legislation establishing youth online safety protections and rules for artificial intelligence.
CT.details: On June 2, 2026, Governor Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15, described as a bipartisan measure addressing youth social media use, artificial intelligence oversight and workforce training. State officials described it as a collaborative effort involving the attorney general and legislators.

IA.title: Iowa governor signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills from the 2026 legislative session.
IA.details: On June 2, 2026, Governor Kim Reynolds took final action on the remaining bills of the 2026 legislative session, signing dozens of measures into law. These were the final bills she signed as governor, as she is not seeking another term in 2026.

NH.title: New Hampshire governor signs and vetoes bills
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law and issued vetoes on several others.
NH.details: In the week of June 12, 2026, Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law covering topics including school district grants and vehicle registration. She also issued veto statements for several measures, including bills related to labor department authority and marijuana cultivation.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable recent developments from cited sources and takes no position on any party, official, or issue.
